title: OS-level filesystem boundary enforcement (no-out-of-scope-work invariant) + WorkPacketScopeBinder
goal: Translate a work-packet's `scope.allowed_paths` / `scope.forbidden_paths` into ProcessSpec BindSpecs so the OS-level filesystem boundary mechanically prevents a sandboxed validation/model process from reading or writing outside the WP scope. Per cluster B subscope: 'strict no out-of-scope work enforcement via OS-level filesystem boundary'. The bind-mount + ReadOnly + denied-by-omission semantics turn the work-packet contract into an OS-enforced gate.
expected_diff_shape: New src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/work_packet_scope_binder.rs that reads .GOV/task_packets/<wp>/packet.json scope, expands glob patterns to concrete host paths, and produces a Vec<BindSpec>; new tests under src/backend/handshake_core/tests/work_packet_scope_binder_tests.rs.
unblock_conditions: Shared types + at least one functional adapter (WSL2Podman) merged so integration tests can exercise the binds end-to-end.
implementation_notes: Algorithm: `pub fn bind_specs_from_packet(packet_path: &Path, repo_root: &Path) -> Result<Vec<BindSpec>, ScopeBinderError>`. Steps: (1) read packet.json, parse `scope.allowed_paths: Vec<String>` (glob patterns rooted at repo_root, e.g. "src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/**"); (2) for each glob, walk the filesystem (globwalk crate or globset+walkdir) and collect concrete host paths; (3) MERGE overlapping concrete paths into their longest-common-prefix directory so the bind set is small (e.g., paths under src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/ become a single bind on that directory); (4) emit each merged path as BindSpec { host_path: <abs path>, guest_path: "/workspace/<relative path>", mode: ReadWrite } — allowed_paths are the ONLY ReadWrite areas inside the guest; (5) ALSO bind read-only the repo root excluding allowed_paths (so the guest can `cargo build` etc but cannot write outside scope) — implement as a BindSpec { host_path: repo_root, guest_path: "/workspace", mode: ReadOnly } FIRST, then layer the allowed_paths ReadWrite binds on top so adapters that honor bind ordering see ReadWrite shadows for in-scope paths and ReadOnly for everything else. forbidden_paths is enforced at binder time: if a path matches both an allowed and a forbidden pattern, ScopeBinderError::ForbiddenPathInAllowed is returned. Note in implementation: this MT does NOT yet wire the binder into ValidationRunner or model_runtime; MT-050 callers receive the binds via the ValidationProcessSpecBuilder. Cross-platform path mapping: on Windows the binds use POSIX-style guest paths (/workspace/...) regardless of host backslash convention; the binder normalizes via `path-slash` crate. Tests: (a) a fixture packet with allowed_paths=["src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/**"] produces exactly one merged ReadWrite bind on the sandbox directory; (b) forbidden_paths overlapping with allowed_paths emits the typed error; (c) deeply nested allowed_paths merge into their common ancestor; (d) integration test on WSL2Podman: spawn a container with the binder's output and verify `touch /workspace/forbidden/x` fails with EROFS while `touch /workspace/src/backend/handshake_core/src/sandbox/x` succeeds.
risk_if_missed: Without binder enforcement the WP scope contract is advisory; a model process or validation runner could write outside the declared allowed_paths and produce 'silent scope creep' defects that the validator only catches by inspecting git diff.
validator_focus: (a) forbidden_paths overlap is rejected; (b) merge-to-common-ancestor doesn't accidentally widen scope; (c) repo-root ReadOnly + allowed_paths ReadWrite layering actually prevents OS-level writes (integration test on WSL2); (d) Windows-host POSIX guest path normalization is correct.

minimum_controls: Forbidden-in-allowed overlap rejection prevents accidental scope widening by operator typo.
Common-ancestor merge MUST NOT bind a directory containing files outside the original allowed_paths set; merge algorithm tested against a fixture that has neighbor files at the merge boundary.
Integration test on WSL2 (not just unit test) proves the OS layer actually enforces the bind modes.

integration_validator_review.verdict_reason: WorkPacketScopeBinder is implemented with allowed_paths/forbidden_paths parsing, common-ancestor merge, ReadOnly repo-root + ReadWrite overlay layering, forbidden-in-allowed rejection, and absolute-path rejection. The OS-level EROFS enforcement test (WSL2 integration) is not present - the bind contract is asserted at the Rust struct level but not at the OS layer.
hbr_unsatisfied: HBR-INT-001 partially: the binder output is replay-deterministic for the same FS state, but no integration test verifies the OS layer honors the binds
